data.content: Use **`dotnet watch test`** — it watches for file changes and re-runs automatically, no manual rebuild needed. Let me start it filtered to your failing test:

toolRequests.name: bash

arguments.description: Start dotnet watch for the failing test

toolRequests.intentionSummary: Start dotnet watch for the failing test

data.reasoningText:
The user wants the fastest workflow for iterating on this failing test. The best approach is `dotnet watch test` with a filter for just that one test — it will automatically re-run when they save changes. Let me start it up so they can see it in action.

The bug is clear: `ProcessPayment` only checks for `== 0` but not for negative amounts. The fix is to change the condition to `amount <= 0`.

Let me start `dotnet watch test` filtered to the specific test, then show the fix.

arguments.description: Start dotnet watch for the failing test

toolCalls.name: bash
toolCalls.args: {"command": "cd /tmp/sv-54ee1e66a03d41c5883ae3e86d61193a && dotnet watch test --filter \"ProcessPayment_NegativeAmount_ThrowsException\" 2>&1", "description": "Start dotnet watch for the failing test", "mode": "async", "shellId": "watch"}

data.content: Good — `dotnet watch` is running and waiting for changes. There are two issues: a missing `using System;` and the actual bug (no negative amount validation). Let me fix both:

arguments.old_str:
if (amount == 0) throw new ArgumentException("Amount cannot be zero");
        // Missing: validation for negative amounts
arguments.new_str: if (amount <= 0) throw new ArgumentException("Amount must be positive");

data.reasoningText: There's a missing `using System;` directive. Let me fix both issues — the missing using and the actual bug — then `dotnet watch` will automatically pick up the changes.
